name: p5.js (Creative Coding Framework)
pricing_notes: Open source (LGPL-2.1); Processing Foundation / Lauren Lee McCarthy

performance.notes: 60fps canvas rendering; performance depends on draw() complexity

agent_readiness.notes: Creative coding and generative art framework. function setup() { createCanvas(400, 400); }. function draw() { background(0); fill(255); circle(mouseX, mouseY, 50); }. Shape: rect(), circle(), ellipse(), line(), triangle(), quad(). Color: fill(), stroke(), background(), noStroke(). Transform: translate(), rotate(), scale(). Text: text(), textSize(), textFont(). Images: loadImage(), image(). Sound: p5.sound addon. Animation: draw() loops at 60fps. Event handlers: mousePressed(), keyPressed(), windowResized(). Noise: noise(). Vector: p5.Vector. DOM: createButton(), createSlider(), createInput(). canvas vs 3D mode: WebGL renderer.

gotchas: Global mode pollutes global namespace — use instance mode (new p5(sketch)) in production
No TypeScript types built-in — use @types/p5 from DefinitelyTyped
Beginners: setup() runs once, draw() runs every frame — common confusion
WebGL mode has limited 2D API — different coordinate system
Large sketches should use preload() for async asset loading
best_when: Creative coding, generative art, and interactive data visualization
Educational contexts for teaching programming through visual output
Prototyping animations and interactive canvas elements
avoid_when: Production UI components (use React/Vue canvas libraries)
High-performance game engines (use PixiJS or Three.js)
When TypeScript type safety is critical
summary: p5.js is a creative coding framework. setup() + draw() lifecycle. shape(), color(), transform() primitives. Mouse/keyboard events. Sound addon. WebGL 3D mode. LGPL-2.1 by Processing Foundation. Excellent docs and educational community. Global mode pollutes namespace — use instance mode. No built-in TypeScript. Best for creative coding, generative art, and visual education. PixiJS for performance games.
